name: Route of the Three Reservoirs of the Geoparque
name_original: Los tres Embalses — circular por la Bolera, el Portillo y San Clemente
description: This difficult 67.7-mile road cycling route circles the Bolera, Portillo, and San Clemente reservoirs, offering challenging climbs and sceni
region_intro: Road cycling around Castilléjar offers diverse terrain within the Altiplano de Granada, characterized by a striking contrast between arid "badlands" and fertile river valleys. The region is part of the Granada Geopark, featuring unique geological formations, canyons, and ravines shaped over millions of years. Cyclists can navigate quiet tarmac roads that traverse both desert-like landscapes and lush areas along the Guardal, Barbatas, and Galera rivers. The area provides a mix of terrain, from gentle slopes to notable ascents, catering to various road cycling preferences. Best road cycling routes around Castilléjar The most popular road cycling route is Route of the Three Reservoirs of the Geoparque, a difficult 67.7 miles (108.9 km) trail that takes 5 hours 15 minutes to complete, offering significant elevation gain through varied landscapes. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Huéscar and Orce Loop, a moderate 56.9 miles (91.5 km) path. This route provides views of the region's distinctive geological formations and traditional agrarian areas. Local road cyclists also love the Castril, Huéscar and Castilléjar, a 45.8 miles (73.7 km) trail leading through river valleys and past local villages, often completed in about 3 hours 20 minutes. Road cycling around Castilléjar is defined by dramatic "badlands" scenery, lush river valleys, and unique geological formations. The network offers options for different ability levels, from moderate rides to more challenging ascents. The guide includes highly-rated routes that showcase the best of the region's landscape. More than 180 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Castilléjar's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What kind of terrain can I expect when road cycling around Castilléjar?
A: The region offers a striking contrast, from arid "badlands" with unique geological formations, canyons, and ravines, to lush river valleys along the Guardal, Barbatas, and Galera rivers. You'll find a mix of quiet tarmac roads with little vehicular traffic, ranging from gentle slopes to notable ascents, providing a dynamic cycling experience.

Q: How many road cycling routes are available in the Castilléjar area?
A: There are over 25 road cycling routes around Castilléjar documented on komoot, catering to various skill levels and preferences. These routes showcase the diverse landscapes of the Granada Geopark.

Q: What are the difficulty levels of road cycling routes around Castilléjar?
A: The routes around Castilléjar offer a range of difficulties. You'll find 2 easy routes for a relaxed ride, 17 moderate routes that provide a good challenge, and 6 difficult routes for experienced cyclists seeking significant elevation gain and longer distances.

Q: What are some notable natural features or landmarks I can see along the cycling routes?
A: Cyclists can explore the dramatic "badlands" scenery and unique geological formations. Highlights include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5297752" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castril Gorge&lt;/a&gt;, the scenic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2551605" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;San Clemente Reservoir&lt;/a&gt;, and the tranquil &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4845237" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Las Presas by the Guardal River&lt;/a&gt;, a perfect picnic spot. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e987061125/route-of-the-three-reservoirs-of-the-geoparque" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Route of the Three Reservoirs of the Geoparque&lt;/a&gt; specifically highlights the region's water bodies.

Q: Are there any cultural or historical sites to explore while cycling?
A: Yes, the region is rich in history. You might pass through unique troglodyte villages with their distinctive cave houses, offering a glimpse into the area's past. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6481401" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Historic Center of Castril&lt;/a&gt; is also a notable point of interest.

Q: Are there any family-friendly road cycling routes in Castilléjar?
A: While many routes involve varied terrain, there are 2 easy routes available that could be suitable for families looking for a more relaxed ride. These routes typically feature gentler slopes and shorter distances, allowing for an enjoyable experience for all.

Q: What is the best time of year for road cycling in Castilléjar?
A: The region's climate makes it suitable for cycling for much of the year. Spring and autumn generally offer the most pleasant temperatures for road cycling, avoiding the peak heat of summer while still enjoying clear skies. Winter can also be an option, though some higher elevation routes might be cooler.

Q: Are there any circular road cycling routes available?
A: Yes, many of the routes around Castilléjar are designed as loops, allowing you to start and end in the same location. Examples include the popular &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e987355623/huescar-and-orce-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Huéscar and Orce Loop&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38167324" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Galera – Cúllar loop from La Alquería&lt;/a&gt;, which offer diverse scenery without retracing your path.

Q: Where can I find challenging climbs for road cycling in the area?
A: For cyclists seeking a challenge, the broader Granada province, including areas near the Sierra Nevada, is known for its demanding ascents. Within the Castilléjar area, routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e987061125/route-of-the-three-reservoirs-of-the-geoparque" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Route of the Three Reservoirs of the Geoparque&lt;/a&gt; and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20123692" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castril Village – Portillo Reservoir loop from Fátima&lt;/a&gt; are rated as difficult and feature significant elevation gains.

Q: What do other road cyclists enjoy most about cycling in Castilléjar?
A: Road cyclists frequently praise the high quality, smooth roads with little vehicular traffic, which makes for a very enjoyable and safe riding experience. The dramatic "badlands" scenery, lush river valleys, and unique geological formations are also consistently highlighted as key attractions, offering visually stunning routes.

Q: Are there places to eat or stay that are cyclist-friendly in Castilléjar?
A: Castilléjar and the surrounding villages offer amenities for visitors. The region is known for its rural tourism, including reconstructed cave houses that serve as unique accommodation. You'll find local cafes and establishments where you can refuel and relax after your rides, often with a focus on traditional agrarian charm.
tips: The municipality of Cortes de Baza is located in the north of the province of Granada. It comes from the Arabic "Qultis" whose meaning was used in the past to refer to a small agricultural and livestock holding, like a country house.
It is a dam built in 1968 and is used to retain irrigation water and for human consumption, since its facilities are not used to produce electricity.

The El Portillo reservoir or El Portillo Dam is located on the Castril River, in the municipality of Castril, in the province of Granada. This reservoir belongs to the Guadalquivir Hydrographic Confederation, in the Granada area.

The San Clemente Reservoir is an impressive water reserve located in Húescar. The water that accumulates in it is used mainly for irrigation. This reservoir offers a beautiful natural landscape surrounded by mountains and a great variety of flora and fauna.

faqs.question: Is this cycling route suitable for beginners?
faqs.answer: No, this route is classified as difficult and requires a very good level of fitness. It involves substantial elevation gains and covers a long distance, making it unsuitable for beginners or those new to road cycling.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on this cycling route?
faqs.answer: The route takes you through mountainous landscapes with impressive natural beauty. While many segments are paved and ideal for road cycling, be prepared for challenging climbs and potentially some unpaved sections, especially on routes requiring good fitness. You'll enjoy stunning views of turquoise reservoir waters contrasting with surrounding forests.

faqs.question: What are the main points of interest along the route?
faqs.answer: The primary draw of this route is cycling around the three magnificent reservoirs: La Bolera Reservoir, Portillo Reservoir, and San Clemente Reservoir. You'll also pass through the settlement of Cortes de Baza, and the Portillo Dam is a prominent feature. The area is also known for birdwatching opportunities.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: Given the challenging nature and potential for varied weather in mountainous regions, spring and autumn generally offer the most pleasant conditions for cycling. Summers can be very hot in Andalusia, and winters might bring colder temperatures or even snow at higher elevations. Always check the local forecast before you go.

faqs.question: Where can I park to access the route?
faqs.answer: While specific parking areas aren't detailed, routes in this region often start from local towns or villages. Consider starting from a settlement like Cortes de Baza or nearby Castril, where you may find public parking options. Always ensure parking is permitted and secure.

faqs.question: Is this cycling route dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: This route is designed for road cycling, which is generally not suitable for dogs to accompany you, especially given its length and difficulty. If you're looking for dog-friendly outdoor activities in the region, consider shorter hiking trails where dogs are permitted and can be safely managed on a leash.

faqs.question: Are there any permits or entrance fees required for cycling in Geoparque de Granada?
faqs.answer: Generally, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required for cycling on public roads and established trails within the Geoparque de Granada. However, always respect local regulations, private property, and any signage you encounter. The Geoparque aims to promote sustainable tourism and appreciation of its unique geological heritage.

faqs.question: Are there places to eat or stay near the route?
faqs.answer: Yes, you can find cafes, pubs, and accommodation options in the towns and villages surrounding the reservoirs, such as Cortes de Baza or nearby Castril. It's advisable to plan your stops in advance, especially during off-peak seasons, as some smaller establishments might have limited hours.

faqs.question: Does this route intersect with other official trails?
faqs.answer: Yes, while primarily a road cycling route, it briefly intersects with sections of other official trails. You'll find short overlaps with paths like Transandalus, Gran Senda Primeros Pobladores (1/6), Gran Senda Primeros Pobladores (2/6), and Sendero Peña de Quesada.

faqs.question: What essential gear should I bring for this challenging ride?
faqs.answer: For this long and difficult racebike route, ensure you have a well-maintained road bike, appropriate cycling attire, a helmet, and plenty of water and nutrition. Given the potential for unpaved sections, consider tires with good puncture resistance. A repair kit, spare tube, pump, and a fully charged phone are also essential. Don't forget sun protection and layers for changing weather.

faqs.question: Is this a circular route or out-and-back?
faqs.answer: This is a circular route, meaning you will start and end at the same point, completing a full loop around the three reservoirs. This offers a varied and continuous scenic experience without retracing your steps.
